regulations.name: EU Energy Efficiency Directive (EED Recast) — Directive (EU) 2023/1791
regulations.summary: Mandatory annual reporting for all EU data centers with installed IT power demand ≥500 kW. Operators must disclose PUE, WUE, Energy Reuse Factor (ERF), and Renewable Energy Factor (REF) to the EU-wide database. First report covering 2023 was due 15 September 2024; from 2025 deadline is 15 May.
regulations.relevance: Any facility ≥500 kW must report PUE and cooling metrics annually. Cooling technology choices directly affect PUE disclosure and regulatory compliance standing.

regulations.name: EU Delegated Regulation (EU) 2024/1364 — Data Centre Sustainability Rating Scheme
regulations.summary: Sets out the common EU-wide rating scheme for data centre sustainability. Specifies exact KPIs and data formats required under EED Article 12 reporting. Covers energy use, water use, waste heat, grid services contribution, and cooling system details.
regulations.relevance: Defines how cooling systems must be documented and rated. Liquid cooling deployments may qualify for higher efficiency ratings under the scheme.

regulations.name: EU Taxonomy Regulation (EU) 2020/852 — Climate Delegated Act (Data Centre Criteria)
regulations.summary: Data center operations qualify as environmentally sustainable only if PUE ≤1.3 for existing facilities or ≤1.2 for new builds from 2025, combined with renewable energy and water thresholds. Misclassification exposes investors and operators to greenwashing liability under SFDR and CSRD.
regulations.relevance: New data centers with liquid cooling are more likely to achieve PUE ≤1.2 and qualify as EU Taxonomy-aligned, enabling access to green financing and lower cost of capital.

regulations.name: Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) — Directive (EU) 2022/2464
regulations.summary: Large companies and listed SMEs must report environmental, social, and governance data under European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS), including Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions. Data center operators above applicable thresholds must disclose energy consumption and efficiency metrics under ESRS E1.
regulations.relevance: Operators deploying liquid cooling to lower PUE and carbon intensity must document and report those gains under CSRD. Failure to improve efficiency metrics increases CSRD disclosure risk.

regulations.name: EU Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (EPBD Recast) — Directive (EU) 2024/1275
regulations.summary: Recast 2024 EPBD requires Member States to enforce minimum energy performance standards for technical building systems including cooling. Data centers in buildings subject to EPBD must demonstrate energy performance compliance and obtain energy performance certificates.
regulations.relevance: New data center buildings must meet minimum energy performance standards. Advanced cooling systems (DLC, immersion) contribute to achieving standards and reduce permitting friction.

regulations.name: Energieeffizienzgesetz (EnEfG) — Act to Increase Energy Efficiency in Germany
regulations.summary: Came into force 18 November 2023. Applies to private data centers ≥1 MW and public data centers ≥300 kW. Requires annual PUE reporting to BAFA by 31 March each year. Mandates 50% renewable energy from 1 January 2024 and 100% from 1 January 2027. Operators must establish energy or environmental management systems by 1 July 2025.
regulations.relevance: Directly mandates PUE measurement and annual disclosure. Liquid cooling investments that lower PUE demonstrate compliance. BAFA registry data is publicly searchable — a lead source for verified facility PUE data.

regulations.name: EnEfG §§ 11-16 — Data Centre Energy Efficiency & Waste Heat (Abwärmenutzung)
regulations.summary: DCs starting after July 2027 must reuse ≥15% of waste heat, after July 2028 ≥20%. Operators must report energy consumption per customer annually. Energy/environmental management system required by July 2025.
regulations.relevance: Liquid and immersion cooling systems produce recoverable waste heat at higher and more usable temperatures than air cooling. New German data centers with liquid cooling are better positioned to meet waste heat utilization targets.

regulations.name: Hessische Bauordnung (HBO) — Hessen State Building Code
regulations.summary: Hessen state building regulation governing construction, structural safety, fire protection, and technical building systems for large industrial and commercial buildings including data centers. Permitting authority is the local Bauamt (building authority).
regulations.relevance: Data center construction and cooling infrastructure (piping, CDUs, immersion tanks) in Frankfurt/Hessen require HBO compliance including fire protection review for liquid cooling systems.

regulations.name: Bundes-Immissionsschutzgesetz (BImSchG) — Federal Immission Control Act
regulations.summary: Large data center facilities may require immission control permits if they exceed noise or emissions thresholds. Cooling towers and large HVAC equipment are subject to TA Lärm noise emission limits. Evaporative cooling towers also require assessment under VDI 2089.
regulations.relevance: Evaporative cooling towers require BImSchG permitting and TA Lärm compliance. Dry closed-loop liquid cooling and immersion systems generate less noise and have a lighter regulatory footprint.

regulations.name: Besluit Kwaliteit Leefomgeving (BKL) Amendment — Hyperscale Data Center Ban
regulations.summary: Amendment effective 1 January 2024 bans new hyperscale data centers (>10 hectares and >70 MW electrical connected load) throughout most of the Netherlands. Exceptions apply only in Het Hogeland municipality (Eemshaven) and Hollands Kroon municipality (Agriport A7).
regulations.relevance: Smaller data centers (<70 MW) or facilities in permitted northern zones are the only viable new-build opportunities. Edge and mid-size facilities with liquid cooling remain the target market.

regulations.name: Amsterdam Municipality — Data Center Policy (Duurzaam Digitaal 2020-2030)
regulations.summary: Amsterdam permanently restricts new hyperscale data centers. New DCs must achieve PUE ≤1.2. Part of Duurzaam Digitaal sustainability framework.
regulations.relevance: Entirely blocks new cooling deployments or retrofits inside Amsterdam city limits. Operators seeking liquid cooling upgrades in the Amsterdam metro must operate in Aalsmeer, Haarlemmermeer, Almere, or permitted northern zones.

regulations.name: Activiteitenbesluit Milieubeheer — Energy Saving Obligation (Energiebesparingsplicht)
regulations.summary: Requires businesses including data centers to implement recognized energy conservation measures with payback period ≤5 years. Enforced by municipalities.
regulations.relevance: Operators must document and implement energy saving measures. Liquid cooling upgrades clearing the 5-year payback threshold qualify as mandated measures that can be reported to RVO as compliance evidence.

regulations.name: Omgevingswet (Environmental Act) — Environmental Permits for Cooling Systems
regulations.summary: Integrated environmental permitting for cooling water discharge, noise, and emissions. Replaced multiple predecessor laws from January 2024.
regulations.relevance: Liquid cooling systems using regulated refrigerants or producing water discharge require environmental permits. Direct-to-chip with open water loops requires water-discharge compliance assessment.

regulations.name: CRU Decision CRU2025236 — Large Energy Users Connection Policy (December 2025)
regulations.summary: Final CRU policy published December 2025 ends the effective moratorium on new data center grid connections. New facilities with MIC ≥1 MVA must: (1) meet 80% of annual electricity demand via additional Irish renewables with a 6-year glide path; (2) provide dispatchable onsite or proximate generation/storage matching MIC; (3) participate in the Single Electricity Market (SEM). De-minimis threshold <1 MVA MIC is exempt.
regulations.relevance: Efficient liquid cooling designs reduce total MIC required for a given IT load (lower PUE = less wasted power), easing compliance with onsite generation matching requirements and improving project economics.

regulations.name: Ireland EED Transposition — Data Centre Energy and Sustainability Performance Reporting
regulations.summary: Ireland transposed EU EED Article 12 requiring operators of data centers ≥500 kW IT load to report PUE, WUE, ERF, and REF annually to the EU database. The Department of Climate, Energy and the Environment administers the obligation. First mandatory report was due 15 September 2024 covering calendar year 2023.
regulations.relevance: Irish data centers ≥500 kW must formally measure and disclose PUE. Liquid cooling investments that improve PUE must be reflected in mandatory annual disclosures and affect Taxonomy alignment assessment.

regulations.name: CRU Decision CRU2025236 — Data Centre Electricity Connection Policy (incl. Dublin Constrained Zone)
regulations.summary: Commission for Regulation of Utilities establishes connection policy for data centres including Dublin grid constraints. Large energy users must demonstrate flexibility and onsite generation.
regulations.relevance: Dublin data center projects with lower total connected load (enabled by efficient liquid cooling) face less grid impact scrutiny. Smaller-footprint, high-efficiency cooling designs improve connection prospects.

regulations.name: Planning and Development Act 2000 (as amended) — Strategic Infrastructure Development
regulations.summary: Data centers meeting thresholds for Strategic Infrastructure Development (SID) — aggregate floor area >50,000 sqm or connection at 110 kV or higher — are subject to An Bord Pleanála review. Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) is required for large facilities, covering cooling water consumption and noise impacts.
regulations.relevance: Large data center projects with substantial cooling plant (CDUs, cooling towers, immersion tanks) undergo SID-level EIA. Closed-loop liquid cooling with minimal water discharge and low noise profile reduces EIA complexity.

regulations.name: Lag (2025:570) om uppgiftsskyldighet om energiprestanda i datacenter — Act on Data Centre Energy Performance Disclosure
regulations.summary: Entered into force 1 July 2025. Requires owners or operators of data centers with installed IT power demand ≥500 kW to report energy performance data (PUE, WUE, ERF, REF) to the EU-wide database under EED Article 12. Operators must notify the Swedish Energy Agency (Energimyndigheten) by 27 April 2026; EU database reporting due 15 May 2026 covering full year 2025.
regulations.relevance: Swedish data centers ≥500 kW must formally measure and disclose PUE annually. Facilities deploying liquid cooling to achieve lower PUE gain a regulatory compliance advantage and may qualify for Taxonomy-aligned financing.

regulations.name: Swedish Energy Tax — Data Center Electricity Tax Rate Changes
regulations.summary: Data centers previously received reduced electricity tax rate. New rules align with EU regulations. Refund type 795 applies. Tax changes effective from 2025.
regulations.relevance: Higher electricity costs amplify the financial return of any PUE improvement. Liquid cooling investments with a 5–10% PUE reduction generate larger cost savings in Sweden than in lower-electricity-cost jurisdictions.

regulations.name: Miljöbalk (Environmental Code) — Chapter 9 Environmentally Hazardous Activities
regulations.summary: Large data centers may require environmental permits under the Miljöbalk if classified as environmentally hazardous activities. Cooling systems using ammonia, CO2, or producing significant water discharge require formal permit applications to County Administrative Boards (Länsstyrelser).
regulations.relevance: Cooling system choice affects permit complexity. Free-air and closed water loop cooling in Nordic climate have low regulatory burden. Immersion systems using novel or flammable dielectric fluids may require chemical risk assessment.

regulations.name: Code de l'énergie Article L.236-2 — Waste Heat Valorization (Law No. 2025-391 of 30 April 2025)
regulations.summary: Enacted 30 April 2025, Law 2025-391 requires data centers with total rated power ≥1 MW to valorize their waste heat through recovery applications unless technically or economically infeasible. Specific conditions, modalities, and exemptions are to be defined by Conseil d'État decree.
regulations.relevance: Liquid cooling systems (especially immersion and DLC with warm water return loops) produce waste heat at higher and more usable temperatures than air cooling. French data centers with liquid cooling are better positioned to comply.

regulations.name: CRE Deliberation No. 2024-229 (18 December 2024) — Grid Connection Framework for Large Energy Users
regulations.summary: From 1 August 2025, CRE's deliberation governs grid connections for new large electricity consumers including data centers. Options: immediate connection at full capacity subject to 5-year reassessment, or progressive ramp-up over up to 10 years. CRE deliberation No. 2025-120 (7 May 2025) adds a fast-track for very large consumers on State-designated favorable sites.
regulations.relevance: Lower connected load via efficient cooling enables faster full-capacity connection. Data centers with liquid cooling achieving PUE ≤1.2 require less total grid import capacity for equivalent IT load.

regulations.name: ICPE (Installations Classées pour la Protection de l'Environnement) — Data Center Environmental Classification
regulations.summary: Data centers in France are classified as ICPEs if they exceed electricity consumption or cooling system thresholds. Most large data centers fall under rubric 2925 (transformers) or 4802 (flammable liquids for immersion cooling dielectrics). ICPE status requires declaration, registration, or full authorization depending on thresholds.
regulations.relevance: Immersion cooling with dielectric fluid may trigger ICPE classification under flammable liquid rubrics (4802), adding permitting steps. Operators must classify fluids and file accordingly before deployment.

regulations.name: Code de l'urbanisme Art. L300-6-2 — Projet d'Intérêt National Majeur (PINM) Fast-Track
regulations.summary: Enables strategic projects including data centers to receive accelerated planning approval. Allows state to override local planning documents for nationally significant infrastructure.
regulations.relevance: Large AI-focused data center projects with advanced liquid cooling infrastructure demonstrating energy efficiency targets are well-positioned to seek PINM designation and accelerate deployment timelines.

regulations.name: Energy Savings Opportunity Scheme (ESOS) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/1643), as amended by SI 2023/1182
regulations.summary: Large UK undertakings (250+ employees or turnover >50M GBP) must conduct energy audits every 4 years and submit action plans. Phase 3 (2024-2028) requires facility-level action plans and progress updates. Covers all energy consumption including building cooling systems.
regulations.relevance: Data centres qualifying as large undertakings must audit cooling energy consumption and identify efficiency opportunities. Phase 3 amendments (SI 2023/1182) tighten reporting with mandatory action plans.

regulations.name: Climate Change Agreements (CCA) for Data Centres Sector
regulations.summary: Voluntary agreements between data centre operators and the Environment Agency to meet energy efficiency targets. Participating sites receive a discount on the Climate Change Levy (CCL). Over 170 data centre sites participate. New scheme from January 2026 sets facility-level targets with a sector improvement target of 14.5% relative to a 2022 baseline by end of 2030.
regulations.relevance: Directly incentivises cooling efficiency improvements in data centres. Facility-level targets from 2026 mean each site must individually demonstrate energy reduction, including from cooling systems.

regulations.name: Streamlined Energy and Carbon Reporting (SECR) -- Companies (Directors' Report) and Limited Liability Partnerships (Energy and Carbon Report) Regulations 2018 (SI 2018/1155)
regulations.summary: Large UK companies and LLPs must disclose annual energy use (kWh), greenhouse gas emissions (tCO2e), and an intensity ratio in their annual directors' report. Applies to quoted companies (global scope) and large unquoted companies (UK scope).
regulations.relevance: Data centre operators meeting the 'large company' threshold must report total energy consumption including cooling. Drives transparency on cooling energy as a share of total facility consumption.

regulations.name: Building Regulations 2010 -- Approved Document L: Conservation of Fuel and Power (Volume 2: Non-domestic Buildings), 2021 edition with 2023 amendments
regulations.summary: Sets minimum energy performance standards for new and refurbished non-domestic buildings in England. Covers building fabric insulation, air tightness, fixed building services including cooling, ventilation, and lighting. Cooling systems must meet minimum efficiency standards (SEER ratings) and new installations must be commissioned to demonstrate compliance.
regulations.relevance: New data centre buildings must comply with Part L energy performance requirements. Cooling system design (including SEER specifications) directly governed. Advanced liquid cooling may help exceed minimum standards.

regulations.name: Environmental Permitting (England and Wales) Regulations 2016 (SI 2016/1154)
regulations.summary: Consolidated environmental permitting regime for regulated activities including water discharge to surface water and groundwater, industrial installations, and waste operations. Permits issued by the Environment Agency with conditions on discharge volumes, quality, temperature, and frequency.
regulations.relevance: Data centres using evaporative or water-based cooling that discharge to surface water or ground require environmental permits. Discharge temperature limits and chemical treatment of cooling water are regulated.

regulations.name: Water Resources Act 1991 -- Abstraction Licensing (as amended by Water Act 2003)
regulations.summary: Abstraction of more than 20 cubic metres per day from any inland water, underground strata, or watercourse requires a licence from the Environment Agency. The Water Act 2003 reformed the licensing system and introduced time-limited licences.
regulations.relevance: Data centres using large volumes of water for evaporative cooling must obtain abstraction licences. Licence conditions may limit volumes and seasonal availability, constraining water-intensive cooling designs.

regulations.name: Water Industry Act 1991, Part IV -- Trade Effluent (Sections 118-141)
regulations.summary: Any discharge of trade effluent (including cooling water blowdown) into public sewers requires a trade effluent consent from the sewerage undertaker. Conditions may specify temperature, flow rate, chemical composition, and monitoring requirements.
regulations.relevance: Cooling tower blowdown and treated cooling water discharged to sewer requires trade effluent consent. Chemical treatment agents (biocides, scale inhibitors) in cooling circuits must comply with discharge conditions.

regulations.name: Fluorinated Greenhouse Gases (Amendment) Regulations 2023 (SI 2023/1161)
regulations.summary: Regulates the use, maintenance, and phase-down of fluorinated greenhouse gases (F-gases) in Great Britain. Requires leak checks on cooling equipment containing F-gases, certified technician requirements, record-keeping, and a progressive HFC phase-down schedule (reduced to 31% of baseline from January 2024).
regulations.relevance: Data centres using traditional refrigerant-based cooling (CRACs, chillers) face increasing constraints on HFC refrigerants. F-gas phase-down drives adoption of low-GWP refrigerants or alternative cooling technologies such as liquid cooling.

regulations.name: Data Centres Designated as Critical National Infrastructure (CNI)
regulations.summary: On 12 September 2024, data centres were designated as Critical National Infrastructure, alongside energy, water, and emergency services. A dedicated CNI data infrastructure team of senior government officials monitors threats, provides prioritised access to the National Cyber Security Centre, and coordinates emergency response. First CNI designation in a decade.
regulations.relevance: Elevates data centres to critical infrastructure status. Cooling system resilience becomes a national security consideration -- failures that threaten data integrity may trigger government intervention protocols.

regulations.name: Nationally Significant Infrastructure Projects (NSIP) -- Data Centres added to Planning Act 2008 regime (December 2024 NPPF reform)
regulations.summary: Data centres above certain thresholds now eligible for the Nationally Significant Infrastructure Projects consenting regime, providing a streamlined planning process. Local planning authorities must consider data centre needs when setting local policies.
regulations.relevance: Streamlined planning for large data centres reduces permitting timelines. Cooling infrastructure (water supply, heat rejection equipment, noise from cooling towers) is assessed as part of the NSIP consent.

regulations.name: Forskrift om datasenter (Data Centre Regulation) -- FOR-2024-12-18-3313
regulations.summary: All data centres with subscribed electrical capacity exceeding 0.5 MW must register with the Norwegian Communications Authority (Nkom) before commencing operations. Operators must report company details, location, services, customer types, cryptocurrency mining share, and total energy consumption. Existing centres had until 1 July 2025 to register. Non-compliance: fines up to 5% of annual turnover.
regulations.relevance: Mandatory registration creates a national inventory of data centres and their energy consumption. Although no direct cooling specifications, the energy reporting requirement encompasses cooling energy use.

regulations.name: Energy Efficiency Assessment Requirement -- Data Centre Strategy 2025 (Chapter 5: Sustainability)
regulations.summary: Companies with average annual energy consumption exceeding 2.5 GWh must prepare implementation plans for energy efficiency measures, present findings to management, and report in annual reports. First assessments due by 1 October 2026. Covers data centres as major energy consumers.
regulations.relevance: Data centres consuming >2.5 GWh/year must formally assess and plan cooling efficiency improvements. Cooling typically represents 30-40% of DC energy, making it a primary target for mandated efficiency planning.

regulations.name: Waste Heat Cost-Benefit Analysis Requirement -- Data Centre Strategy 2025
regulations.summary: Data centre operators with facilities exceeding 2 MW must conduct cost-benefit analyses for excess heat utilisation and must ensure surplus energy from existing and new facilities is utilised internally to the greatest extent possible. NVE approval required before construction of new facilities.
regulations.relevance: Directly mandates that operators evaluate waste heat recovery from cooling systems. Liquid cooling systems produce higher-grade waste heat (40-60C) more suitable for district heating than air-cooled systems.

regulations.name: Electrical Power Tax -- General Rate Applied to Data Centres (removal of reduced rate)
regulations.summary: The reduced electricity tax rate for data centres (introduced 2016) was abolished effective 1 January 2023. Data centres are now subject to the general electricity tax rate (7.13 ore/kWh for 2026). The reduced rate (0.60 ore/kWh) applies only to manufacturing and mining. Estimated additional revenue: 150 million NOK.
regulations.relevance: Loss of the reduced electricity tax rate increases operating costs for data centres, strengthening the business case for energy-efficient cooling technologies that reduce total electricity consumption.

regulations.name: Pollution Control Act (Forurensningsloven) -- Act of 13 March 1981 No. 6
regulations.summary: Fundamental principle: all pollution is prohibited unless specifically permitted. Activities that may cause pollution to air, water, or soil require emission permits from the Norwegian Environment Agency or county governor. Criminal penalties: fines or imprisonment up to 5 years for violations risking human life/health.
regulations.relevance: Data centres with water discharge from cooling systems, backup generator emissions, or refrigerant leaks may require pollution permits. Cooling water thermal discharge to waterways is a regulated pollutant.

regulations.name: Water Resources Act (Vannressursloven) -- Act of 24 November 2000 No. 82
regulations.summary: Governs the use, management, and protection of freshwater resources including rivers, lakes, and groundwater. Abstraction and use of water resources for industrial purposes requires permits from NVE (Norwegian Water Resources and Energy Directorate). Aims to safeguard proper public use of water resources.
regulations.relevance: Data centres using freshwater for cooling (direct water cooling, evaporative towers) must obtain water abstraction permits from NVE. Groundwater use for geothermal cooling also regulated.

regulations.name: Building Technical Regulations (TEK17) -- Forskrift om tekniske krav til byggverk
regulations.summary: National building regulations setting mandatory energy performance requirements for new construction. Office buildings limited to 115 kWh/m2 annual energy consumption. Includes requirements for insulation, air tightness, heating/cooling systems, ventilation, and lighting. From 2022, mandatory greenhouse gas accounting (Section 17-1) for larger buildings.
regulations.relevance: New data centre buildings must meet TEK17 energy performance standards. Cooling system design directly affects compliance with annual energy consumption limits and mandatory GHG accounting.

regulations.name: Energy Act (Energiloven) -- Act of 29 June 1990 No. 50 -- Grid Connection Requirements
regulations.summary: Regulates generation, conversion, transmission, trading, distribution, and use of energy. Grid connection for large consumers requires maturity assessment by grid operator (NEM Section 3-4, effective 1 January 2025). Area licences required for grid installations. NVE oversees grid connection codes (RfG, DCC, HVDC).
regulations.relevance: Data centres seeking grid connection (often 10-100+ MW) face maturity assessments and potential queue delays. 2,691 MW currently reserved for data centres with 4,390 MW awaiting connection. Grid access constraints directly affect cooling capacity planning.

regulations.name: Act on Excise Duty on Electricity and Certain Fuels (1260/1996) -- Data Centre Electricity Tax Category Change
regulations.summary: Data centres currently benefit from the lower electricity tax Category II (0.05 cents/kWh). Effective 1 July 2026, data centres will be moved to the general Category I rate (2.24 cents/kWh), an increase of 2.19 cents/kWh. A compensatory state aid scheme provides 10-year tax relief capped at EUR 30 million for qualifying facilities that reuse waste heat or exceed efficiency standards.
regulations.relevance: The tax increase from Category II to Category I significantly raises operating costs. Facilities deploying efficient cooling systems that enable waste heat reuse qualify for the compensatory 10-year tax relief, directly incentivising liquid cooling and heat recovery.

regulations.name: Energy Efficiency Act (1429/2014) -- Mandatory Energy Audits for Large Enterprises
regulations.summary: Large companies (250+ employees or turnover >EUR 50M and balance sheet >EUR 43M) must conduct comprehensive energy audits every 4 years covering all locations. Audits identify energy consumption profiles and efficiency opportunities. Exemptions for facilities with ISO 50001 or ISO 14001+ETJ+ certification. Enforced by the Energy Authority (Energiavirasto).
regulations.relevance: Large data centre operators must audit cooling energy consumption quadrennially. Cooling systems are typically the largest non-IT energy consumer and primary target for audit-identified savings.

regulations.name: EU Energy Efficiency Directive (EED) National Implementation -- Data Centre Reporting via Energy Authority
regulations.summary: Data centres with installed IT power demand of 500 kW or more must report annually to the European database. KPIs include PUE, WUE, Energy Reuse Factor, Renewable Energy Factor, temperature set points, and cooling system details. The Finnish Energy Authority (Energiavirasto) is the national coordinator. First reporting cycle began in 2024.
regulations.relevance: Mandatory annual PUE and WUE reporting directly measures cooling efficiency. Operators must disclose cooling technology details, creating regulatory pressure for best-in-class cooling systems.

regulations.name: Data Centre Registration Obligation and State Aid Scheme (Ministerial Committee Decision, 5 December 2025)
regulations.summary: A cross-administrative registration system for data centres will be established. To qualify for the 10-year state aid (tax relief), data centres must register AND either reuse waste heat or exceed current energy efficiency standards. Security authorities gain examination powers over data centre investments. Implementation from 1 July 2026.
regulations.relevance: State aid is conditioned on waste heat reuse or high energy efficiency. This directly incentivises cooling architectures (liquid cooling, heat pumps) that produce recoverable waste heat for district heating networks.

regulations.name: Environmental Protection Act (527/2014)
regulations.summary: Activities involving a risk of pollution require an environmental permit from the Regional State Administrative Agency (AVI). Covers emissions to air, water, and soil. Permits specify emission limits, monitoring requirements, and operational conditions. Government Decree on Environmental Protection (713/2014) defines activities subject to permitting.
regulations.relevance: Data centres discharging cooling water, operating backup generators, or using chemical water treatment require environmental permits from AVI. Permit conditions may constrain cooling water discharge temperatures and volumes.

regulations.name: Environmental Permits for Water Activities -- Regional State Administrative Agency (AVI)
regulations.summary: Environmental permits for water abstraction and discharge are processed by Regional State Administrative Agencies (AVI). Activities with extensive environmental impact require AVI permits under both the Environmental Protection Act and the Water Act. Public consultation required for permit applications.
regulations.relevance: Data centres using significant water volumes for cooling must obtain AVI permits for both water abstraction and discharge. Finland's abundant freshwater resources make water-cooled systems feasible, but permits regulate volumes and thermal impact.

regulations.name: Land Use and Building Act (132/1999) -- National Building Code Energy Performance Requirements
regulations.summary: Sets general conditions for building including energy efficiency requirements. Compliance with minimum energy efficiency must be demonstrated by calculations based on energy use, energy loss, and form of energy. The National Building Code of Finland (administered by Ministry of the Environment) provides detailed technical requirements for insulation, HVAC systems, and energy performance.
regulations.relevance: New data centre construction must comply with national building code energy performance requirements. Cooling system efficiency and heat recovery provisions are integral to energy performance calculations.

regulations.name: Electricity Act No. 65/2003
regulations.summary: Governs the generation, transmission, distribution, and sale of electricity in Iceland. A licence from the National Energy Authority (now the Environment and Energy Agency, established January 2025 under Act 110/2024) is required to construct and operate a power plant. Environmental factors must be considered when issuing licences (Article 5).
regulations.relevance: Data centres connected to Iceland's grid benefit from nearly 100% renewable electricity (hydro and geothermal). The Act governs the electricity supply framework that makes Iceland attractive for data centres, though no DC-specific efficiency mandates exist.

regulations.name: Environmental Impact Assessment Act No. 106/2000
regulations.summary: Projects which may involve significant environmental effects require environmental impact assessment before consent is granted. The National Planning Agency decides whether projects are subject to EIA. All major industrial installations and power plants require EIA. Objectives: ensure environmental assessment, encourage stakeholder cooperation, and make environmental information public.
regulations.relevance: Large data centre projects may trigger EIA requirements based on magnitude of environmental effects, including water use, thermal discharge, and land use impacts from cooling infrastructure.

regulations.name: Act on Hygiene and Pollution Prevention No. 7/1998 (replaced by Act No. 25)
regulations.summary: Establishes a permitting system for activities that may cause pollution. Purpose: create healthy living conditions and protect unpolluted environment. Permits reviewed at least every 16 years. Covers water discharge, air emissions, and waste management. Conditions on water reinjection prevent pollution of cold aquifers used for public consumption.
regulations.relevance: Data centres discharging warm cooling water or using geothermal water for cooling must comply with pollution prevention permits. Water reinjection conditions protect Iceland's groundwater resources used for drinking water.

regulations.name: Water Management Act (Vatnalög) -- implementing EU Water Framework Directive
regulations.summary: Provides holistic protection for water and ecosystems covering surface water, groundwater, estuaries, and coastal water. Establishes coordinated management of water resources for protection and sustainable use. The Environment and Energy Agency implements the Act in cooperation with local authorities. River Basin Management Plans (2022-2027) set water quality objectives.
regulations.relevance: Data centres using water for cooling are subject to water management objectives. Iceland's abundant cold water resources are advantageous for direct water cooling, but abstraction and thermal discharge must comply with River Basin Management Plans.

regulations.name: Building Regulation (Byggingarreglugerð) No. 112/2012
regulations.summary: National building regulations for Iceland. Energy efficiency requirements cover insulation, air permeability, and technical building systems including heating and cooling. Heating and cooling systems must conform to specifications in the Icelandic standard IST 66. Designers must ensure energy consumption efficiency throughout the building lifecycle.
regulations.relevance: New data centre buildings must comply with energy performance requirements. Iceland's near-100% renewable energy and cold climate reduce cooling energy needs, but building systems (including cooling) must still meet IST 66 standards.

regulations.name: Act on Working Environment, Health and Safety in Workplaces No. 46/1980 (as amended 2018)
regulations.summary: Ensures safe and healthy working conditions in all workplaces. Employers must prepare written Health and Safety Plans. The Administration of Occupational Safety and Health (AOSH/Vinnueftirlit rikisins) conducts inspections and enforces standards for workplace safety, including temperature, ventilation, and hazardous substances.
regulations.relevance: Data centre cooling systems using refrigerants or operating in confined spaces must comply with workplace safety standards. Temperature and ventilation requirements for occupied areas of data centres are governed by this Act.

regulations.name: Competitive Electricity Pricing (no specific DC tax incentive legislation -- structural advantage)
regulations.summary: Iceland has no specific data centre electricity tax incentive because industrial electricity prices are already among the lowest in Europe due to abundant renewable hydropower and geothermal energy. Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs) are negotiated directly with Landsvirkjun (national power company) at competitive rates. No carbon tax on electricity generation since generation is nearly 100% renewable.
regulations.relevance: Low electricity costs reduce the economic burden of energy-intensive cooling. Combined with cold ambient temperatures, Iceland's structural energy advantage makes it uniquely suitable for data centres without needing explicit tax incentives.

regulations.name: Electricity Tax Act Amendment (Lov nr 1775 af 29/12/2025) -- Reduction to EU Minimum Rate
regulations.summary: Amends the Electricity Tax Act (Elafgiftsloven) to reduce the general electricity tax from 72.7 ore/kWh to 0.8 ore/kWh (the EU minimum rate) for 2026 and 2027. Applies to all electricity consumers including data centres. Enacted 29 December 2025, effective 1 January 2026.
regulations.relevance: Dramatically reduces electricity costs for data centre cooling operations in 2026-2027. The 99% reduction in electricity tax improves the economics of all cooling technologies, but is temporary (2 years).

regulations.name: EU Energy Efficiency Directive (EED) -- Danish National Implementation (via Danish Energy Agency)
regulations.summary: Denmark implements the EED recast requiring data centres with installed IT power demand of 500 kW or more to report PUE, WUE, Energy Reuse Factor, temperature set points, and cooling details annually to the EU database. The Danish Energy Agency (Energistyrelsen) is the national coordinator.
regulations.relevance: Mandatory PUE and WUE reporting creates transparency on cooling efficiency. Danish data centres must disclose cooling technology details annually, driving adoption of efficient cooling systems.

regulations.name: Heat Supply Act (Varmeforsyningsloven) -- Surplus Heat Price Cap Removal (2025 amendment)
regulations.summary: The Heat Supply Act governs Denmark's district heating infrastructure. A 2025 amendment removes the pricing cap on surplus heat, enabling data centres to sell waste heat to district heating networks at market-determined prices. Previously, the price cap reduced the economic incentive for data centres to invest in heat recovery infrastructure. Municipal heat planning ensures integration.
regulations.relevance: Removal of the surplus heat price cap directly incentivises data centres to recover and sell waste heat from cooling systems. Higher-temperature waste heat from liquid cooling systems (40-60C) is more economically valuable for district heating.

regulations.name: Environmental Protection Act (Miljobeskyttelsesloven) -- Chapter 5: Environmental Approval for Listed Activities
regulations.summary: Polluting businesses listed in Appendices 1 and 2 of the Environmental Approval Statutory Order (Godkendelsesbekendtgorelsen) must obtain environmental approval before establishment, changes, or expansion. Data centre backup power plants with rated thermal input of 50 MW or more are listed under Appendix 1, point 1.1b. Municipal or Miljostyrelsen approval depending on listing category.
regulations.relevance: Data centres with large backup generator capacity (50+ MW thermal) require full environmental approval. Smaller facilities may need simplified approval. Cooling water discharge and air emissions from cooling towers also assessed.

regulations.name: Water Supply Act (Vandforsyningsloven) -- LBK nr 1149 af 28/10/2024
regulations.summary: Governs utilisation and protection of water resources based on integrated planning and quality requirements. Municipal councils are responsible for water extraction permits and supply planning. Public water supply facilities have supply obligations within their natural areas. The latest consolidated version was issued 28 October 2024.
regulations.relevance: Data centres using municipal water supply for cooling must comply with water supply planning. Large-scale water extraction for cooling may require coordination with municipal water supply plans and extraction permits.

regulations.name: Building Regulations 2018 (Bygningsreglementet BR18)
regulations.summary: Danish national building code setting standards for energy performance, fire safety, indoor climate, and structural safety. Energy frame limits maximum permitted energy consumption (heating, cooling, ventilation, lighting) per m2. From 2025, buildings with heating or cooling demand above 290 kW must have building automation to control and monitor HVAC systems.
regulations.relevance: Data centre buildings with cooling demand >290 kW (essentially all commercial DCs) must install building automation for cooling control and monitoring. Energy frame calculations must account for cooling energy consumption.

regulations.name: Climate Act (Klimaloven) -- Act No. 965 of 26 June 2020
regulations.summary: Legally binding targets: 70% GHG emission reduction by 2030 (vs 1990) and climate neutrality by 2050 (advanced to 2045 in 2022 coalition agreement). Climate targets set every 5 years with a 10-year perspective. All sectors including data centres expected to contribute to national reduction pathway.
regulations.relevance: Denmark's aggressive climate targets create policy pressure for data centres to minimise emissions from cooling. Operators are expected to demonstrate alignment with national decarbonisation through efficient cooling, waste heat reuse, and renewable energy use.

regulations.name: Energy Efficiency Roadmap (Energistyrelsen/Danish Energy Agency, 2025)
regulations.summary: The Danish Energy Agency's roadmap for energy efficiency includes specific attention to data centres as growing energy consumers. Primary energy consumption is projected to remain stable partly due to data centre growth being offset by efficiency improvements. The roadmap supports waste heat integration with district heating and energy-efficient technology adoption.
regulations.relevance: Policy roadmap signals increasing regulatory attention to data centre energy efficiency. Data centres expected to contribute to national efficiency targets through improved cooling systems and waste heat recovery.

regulations.name: EU EED Recast (Directive 2023/1791) — Direct Applicability via EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 (Data Centre Reporting)
regulations.summary: EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 is directly applicable in Spain without national transposition. All data centre operators with installed IT power demand >=500 kW must report annually to the EU database: PUE, WUE, energy reuse factor (ERF), renewable energy factor (REF), cooling system details, and installed capacity. First report was due 15 September 2024; subsequent reports due 15 May annually. Spain has not yet enacted a specific national transposition of EED Article 12 as of April 2026 — MITECO opened a public consultation in mid-2024 but no Royal Decree has been published.
regulations.relevance: Directly applicable to any DC facility >=500 kW in Spain. Cooling system type, PUE, and WUE are mandatory disclosure items. Liquid cooling deployments produce better reportable metrics.

regulations.name: Real Decreto-ley 14/2022 — Medidas de ahorro y eficiencia energetica
regulations.summary: Royal Decree-Law 14/2022 of 1 August 2022 introduced emergency energy savings and efficiency measures, including mandatory temperature set-points for commercial and public buildings (air-conditioning no lower than 27C in summer), energy audits for large consumers, and support for renewable energy deployment. Partially transposes earlier EED obligations.
regulations.relevance: Temperature set-points apply to common areas of DC facilities open to the public. Large energy consumers including DCs may face enhanced audit obligations. Liquid cooling systems are exempt from air-temperature set-point rules as they do not rely on ambient air conditioning.

regulations.name: Real Decreto 390/2021 — Certificacion de eficiencia energetica de edificios (as amended by RD 659/2025)
regulations.summary: Establishes the basic procedure for energy performance certification of buildings. As amended by Real Decreto 659/2025, buildings including data centres must obtain an energy performance certificate (EPC). The certificate rates the building's energy class (A-G) and must be renewed every 10 years.
regulations.relevance: New-build and major-renovation DC facilities require an EPC. Efficient cooling systems improve the energy class rating, potentially qualifying for 'A' or 'B' class.

regulations.name: Codigo Tecnico de la Edificacion (CTE) — Documento Basico DB-HE Ahorro de Energia (Real Decreto 314/2006, as amended by RD 732/2019 and RD 450/2022)
regulations.summary: Spain's Technical Building Code sets minimum energy performance requirements for all new buildings and major renovations. DB-HE sections cover thermal envelope limits (HE1), HVAC efficiency (HE2), DHW (HE4), and minimum renewable energy contribution (HE5). RD 732/2019 updated nZEB requirements. RD 450/2022 added electric vehicle infrastructure provisions.
regulations.relevance: Data centre new builds must comply with CTE DB-HE for HVAC efficiency, thermal insulation, and renewable energy share. Advanced cooling systems directly affect HE2 compliance.

regulations.name: Real Decreto Legislativo 1/2001 — Texto Refundido de la Ley de Aguas
regulations.summary: Consolidated Water Law. Any private use of public-domain water (surface or groundwater) for industrial cooling requires an administrative concession (concesion administrativa) from the relevant River Basin Authority (Confederacion Hidrografica). Concessions are granted for a maximum of 75 years and specify volume limits, quality return conditions, and fees (canon de regulacion). Existing rights must be registered in the Water Registry.
regulations.relevance: DCs using evaporative cooling towers or water-based cooling drawing from public water sources must obtain a water concession. Closed-loop liquid cooling systems using municipal supply may only need a supply contract, not a concession.

regulations.name: Real Decreto 1620/2007 — Regimen juridico de la reutilizacion de aguas depuradas
regulations.summary: Establishes the legal framework for reuse of treated wastewater. Permits use of reclaimed water in cooling towers and evaporative condensers (Quality Category 3.1) subject to specific microbiological and chemical quality thresholds. Requires an administrative authorization from the River Basin Authority.
regulations.relevance: DCs seeking to reduce freshwater consumption by using reclaimed water for cooling must comply with quality thresholds under Category 3.1. Relevant for evaporative cooling deployments in water-scarce Spanish regions.

regulations.name: Real Decreto Legislativo 1/2016 — Texto Refundido de la Ley de Prevencion y Control Integrados de la Contaminacion (IPPC)
regulations.summary: Consolidated text of the Integrated Pollution Prevention and Control Law, transposing the EU Industrial Emissions Directive (2010/75/EU). Installations listed in Annex I (including combustion plants >=50 MW thermal) require an Integrated Environmental Authorisation (Autorizacion Ambiental Integrada, AAI) covering air emissions, water discharges, waste, noise, and energy efficiency. DC backup generators alone typically fall below Annex I thresholds, but large combined heat/power or district heating schemes may trigger AAI.
regulations.relevance: Most standalone DCs do not trigger AAI, but facilities with large diesel generator banks or co-located industrial activities should verify against Annex I thresholds. Waste heat export schemes may bring DCs into scope.

regulations.name: Ley 50/1985 — Incentivos regionales para correccion de desequilibrios economicos interterritoriales
regulations.summary: Provides regional investment incentives (capital grants of 10-40% of eligible investment) for projects in designated Economic Promotion Zones, Declining Industrial Zones, and Special Zones. Eligible sectors include industrial installations and technology infrastructure. Incentives are awarded by the Ministry of Finance via annual Orders (most recent: Orden HFP/1436/2023). Canary Islands have a separate, more generous regime under Ley 19/1994 (Zona Especial Canaria, ZEC) with a reduced 4% corporate tax rate.
regulations.relevance: DC investments in designated Spanish regions (Andalucia, Extremadura, Galicia, Canarias, etc.) may qualify for capital grants or tax credits. ZEC in Canary Islands offers 4% corporate tax for qualifying entities.

regulations.name: EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 — Direct Effect in Spain (PUE Reporting)
regulations.summary: PUE reporting for Spanish DCs >=500 kW is mandated directly by EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364, which is directly applicable without national transposition. Operators must report PUE calculated per EN 50600-4-2, along with WUE (EN 50600-4-9), ERF, REF, and cooling system type. Reports go to the EU database managed by the European Commission. Spain has no additional national PUE reporting obligation beyond the EU requirement as of April 2026.
regulations.relevance: PUE reporting is a direct EU obligation for DCs >=500 kW. No separate Spanish reporting layer exists yet.

regulations.name: Decreto Legislativo 4 luglio 2014, n. 102 — Attuazione della direttiva sull'efficienza energetica (as amended)
regulations.summary: Italy's primary energy efficiency law, transposing Directive 2012/27/EU and subsequently updated for EED Recast obligations. Mandates energy audits every 4 years for large enterprises (>250 employees or >50M EUR turnover). Companies consuming >85 TJ/year must adopt ISO 50001 by 11 October 2027. Companies between 10-85 TJ/year must conduct quadrennial energy audits from 2026. Data centres are explicitly covered as large energy consumers. Italy missed the 11 October 2025 deadline for full transposition of Directive 2023/1791; a draft legislative decree was under consultation in 2025-2026.
regulations.relevance: Large DCs likely exceed the 10 TJ/year threshold and face mandatory energy audits or ISO 50001 adoption. Cooling system efficiency is a key audit parameter.

regulations.name: EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 — Direct Applicability (Data Centre Reporting)
regulations.summary: Directly applicable in Italy. Data centre operators with installed IT power demand >=500 kW must report PUE, WUE, ERF, REF, cooling system details, and installed capacity to the EU database. First report due 15 September 2024; annual reports due 15 May thereafter. Italy has designated ENEA (Agenzia nazionale per le nuove tecnologie, l'energia e lo sviluppo economico sostenibile) as the national contact point.
regulations.relevance: All Italian DCs >=500 kW must report PUE and cooling metrics annually to the EU database via ENEA.

regulations.name: DPR 6 giugno 2001, n. 380 — Testo Unico delle disposizioni in materia edilizia
regulations.summary: Italy's Consolidated Building Code. All new construction and major renovations require a building permit (Permesso di Costruire) from the municipal authority (Comune). The permit process involves urban planning compliance, structural safety, fire prevention, and energy performance verification. Data centres as commercial/industrial buildings require permits under Title II. Seismic zone compliance (Part II, Title IV) applies in much of Italy.
regulations.relevance: DC new-builds require Permesso di Costruire. Cooling system design affects structural load calculations and fire prevention assessments. Seismic compliance is critical in central and southern Italy.

regulations.name: Decreto Legislativo 3 aprile 2006, n. 152 — Testo Unico Ambientale (Codice dell'Ambiente)
regulations.summary: Italy's comprehensive Environmental Code. Part II covers EIA (VIA) and Integrated Environmental Authorisation (AIA/IPPC). Part III covers water protection and management, including water discharge permits. Part IV covers waste. Part V covers air quality. Large industrial installations listed in Annex VIII (combustion >50 MW thermal, etc.) require AIA from the Ministry or Region. DCs with significant backup generation or industrial cooling water discharge may fall within scope. Water abstraction for cooling requires authorization under Part III.
regulations.relevance: DCs with large diesel backup generators should check Annex VIII thresholds. Water-cooled facilities discharging thermal wastewater need authorization under Part III. EIA may be required for very large DC projects depending on regional thresholds.

regulations.name: Regio Decreto 11 dicembre 1933, n. 1775 — Testo Unico sulle acque pubbliche (as amended by D.Lgs. 152/2006)
regulations.summary: Italy's foundational water law, still in force as amended. Industrial water abstraction (derivazione) from public watercourses or groundwater requires a concession from the Region or River Basin District Authority. The concession specifies maximum flow rates, quality return conditions, and fees (canoni). Processing time is 6-18 months. Large abstractions (>100 l/s) require additional environmental screening.
regulations.relevance: DCs drawing water from rivers, lakes, or wells for cooling require a concession. Closed-loop liquid cooling using municipal water supply avoids this, but open-loop or evaporative systems do not.

regulations.name: Piano Transizione 5.0 — Art. 38 Decreto-Legge 2 marzo 2024, n. 19 (converted by Legge 29 aprile 2024, n. 56)
regulations.summary: Transizione 5.0 provides a tax credit for enterprises making investments in production structures located in Italy during 2024-2025 that result in measurable energy consumption reductions. The credit ranges from 5% to 45% depending on investment size and energy savings achieved. Eligible investments include digital transformation assets (Industry 4.0 goods), energy efficiency upgrades, and self-generation from renewables. Funded with 1,039.5M EUR (2024), 3,118.5M EUR (2025), and 415.8M EUR/year (2026-2030). Extended and modified by Legge 4/2026.
regulations.relevance: DC operators investing in energy-efficient cooling systems (liquid cooling, heat recovery) may qualify for Transizione 5.0 tax credits of 5-45% if the investment produces measurable energy savings. Self-generation (solar, etc.) at DC sites is also eligible.

regulations.name: EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 — Direct Effect in Italy (PUE Reporting)
regulations.summary: PUE reporting for Italian DCs >=500 kW is mandated directly by EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364. PUE must be calculated per EN 50600-4-2 and reported alongside WUE, ERF, REF, and cooling system type to the EU database. ENEA manages the Italian reporting portal. No additional national PUE reporting obligation exists beyond the EU requirement, though the pending full EED transposition may add national-layer requirements.
regulations.relevance: Annual PUE disclosure is mandatory for DCs >=500 kW. Italy may add further national reporting requirements upon completing EED 2023/1791 transposition.

regulations.name: Ustawa z dnia 20 maja 2016 r. o efektywnosci energetycznej (Energy Efficiency Act, consolidated text 2024 — Dz.U. 2024 poz. 1047)
regulations.summary: Poland's primary energy efficiency law, transposing the original EED (2012/27/EU). Mandates energy audits for large enterprises every 4 years. Establishes energy savings obligations and a white certificate trading system. The consolidated text was published 21 June 2024. As of April 2026, Poland has not yet completed transposition of the EED Recast (Directive 2023/1791) — the 11 October 2025 deadline has passed. The EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 on DC reporting is directly applicable regardless.
regulations.relevance: Large DCs in Poland face mandatory energy audits. The white certificate system can generate tradeable certificates for energy efficiency investments including cooling upgrades.

regulations.name: EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 — Direct Applicability in Poland (Data Centre Reporting)
regulations.summary: Directly applicable in Poland. All DC operators with installed IT power demand >=500 kW must report PUE, WUE, ERF, REF, cooling system type, and capacity to the EU database. First report due 15 September 2024; annual reports due 15 May thereafter. Poland's national regulatory framework for administering these reports is still being developed.
regulations.relevance: Polish DCs >=500 kW must report PUE and cooling metrics to the EU database annually.

regulations.name: Prawo budowlane (Building Law, consolidated text 2024 — Dz.U. 2024 poz. 725) and Rozporzadzenie w sprawie warunkow technicznych (Technical Conditions Regulation, Dz.U. 2024 poz. 726)
regulations.summary: Poland's Building Law requires a building permit (pozwolenie na budowe) for commercial/industrial construction including data centres. The Technical Conditions Regulation (Rozporzadzenie Ministra Rozwoju i Technologii z dnia 9 maja 2024 r.) sets minimum requirements for thermal insulation, HVAC efficiency, energy performance, and ventilation. The 2024 amendments tightened nZEB requirements for non-residential buildings.
regulations.relevance: DC new-builds require building permits and must comply with updated thermal and HVAC efficiency standards. Cooling system design documentation is part of the permit application.

regulations.name: Ustawa o charakterystyce energetycznej budynkow (Act on Building Energy Performance, consolidated text 2024 — Dz.U. 2024 poz. 101)
regulations.summary: Transposes the EPBD into Polish law. Requires energy performance certificates for new buildings, buildings sold or rented, and public buildings >250 m2. Large commercial buildings (including DCs) above threshold must display energy certificates. The 2022 amendment (Dz.U. 2022 poz. 2206) expanded digital registry requirements.
regulations.relevance: DC facilities require energy performance certificates. Cooling efficiency directly affects the certificate class.

regulations.name: Prawo wodne (Water Law, consolidated text 2024 — Dz.U. 2024 poz. 1087)
regulations.summary: Poland's Water Law (Ustawa z dnia 20 lipca 2017 r.) requires a water-law permit (pozwolenie wodnoprawne) for industrial water abstraction exceeding 5 m3/day or water discharge to surface waters. Permits are issued by the Polish Waters Authority (Wody Polskie). Thermal discharge to watercourses is subject to temperature limits. Water use fees are calculated per m3 abstracted.
regulations.relevance: DCs using open-loop cooling or abstracting groundwater/surface water for cooling towers need a pozwolenie wodnoprawne. Closed-loop liquid cooling using municipal supply typically does not trigger this requirement.

regulations.name: Prawo ochrony srodowiska (Environmental Protection Law, consolidated text 2025 — Dz.U. 2025 poz. 647)
regulations.summary: Poland's comprehensive environmental protection law. Part IV establishes integrated permits (pozwolenie zintegrowane) for installations listed in Annex to the Act (transposing the Industrial Emissions Directive). Combustion plants >=50 MW thermal, certain waste management operations, and chemical plants require integrated permits. DCs with large backup generator installations should verify against Annex thresholds. The 2024 amendment (Dz.U. 2024 poz. 1940) updated ETS provisions.
regulations.relevance: Most standalone DCs fall below integrated permit thresholds, but large installations with backup power capacity approaching 50 MW thermal should verify. Noise emission limits in the law apply to all DC facilities.

regulations.name: Ustawa o wspieraniu nowych inwestycji (Act on Supporting New Investments — Polish Investment Zone, Dz.U. 2018 poz. 1162) and Rozporzadzenie RM z dnia 27 grudnia 2022 r. (Dz.U. 2022 poz. 2861)
regulations.summary: Replaced the old Special Economic Zones regime with a nationwide Polish Investment Zone (Polska Strefa Inwestycji). Qualifying new investments receive a corporate income tax exemption (CIT at 0% on income from the investment) for 10-15 years, depending on region and investment size. Minimum investment thresholds vary by region (from 200,000 PLN in less-developed areas to 100M PLN in Warsaw). The 2022 Council of Ministers Regulation specifies eligible activities and maximum aid intensity (10-50% of eligible costs).
regulations.relevance: DC investments in Poland can qualify for CIT exemptions under the Polish Investment Zone, particularly in eastern and southeastern Poland where aid intensity reaches 50%. The exemption covers income from the qualifying investment for 10-15 years.

regulations.name: EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 — Direct Effect in Poland (PUE Reporting)
regulations.summary: PUE reporting for Polish DCs >=500 kW is mandated directly by EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364. PUE must be reported per EN 50600-4-2 to the EU database. Poland has not yet established a national-layer PUE reporting obligation beyond the EU requirement.
regulations.relevance: Annual PUE disclosure mandatory for DCs >=500 kW via EU database.

regulations.name: Decreto-Lei n.o 118/2013 — Sistema de Certificacao Energetica (SCE) and RECS (as amended by DL 101-D/2020)
regulations.summary: Establishes Portugal's Building Energy Certification System (SCE), the Energy Performance Regulation for Residential Buildings (REH), and the Energy Performance Regulation for Commercial and Service Buildings (RECS). Large commercial buildings (>=1000 m2, or >=500 m2 for specific categories) must obtain energy certificates and implement energy management plans. Data centres as commercial/service buildings fall under RECS. Amended by Decreto-Lei 101-D/2020 to transpose EPBD Recast requirements including nZEB standards. ADENE (Agencia para a Energia) manages the SCE.
regulations.relevance: DCs above 1000 m2 or using significant HVAC energy must obtain SCE certification under RECS. Cooling system efficiency is a key variable in the energy certificate rating.

regulations.name: EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 — Direct Applicability in Portugal (Data Centre Reporting)
regulations.summary: Directly applicable in Portugal. All DC operators with installed IT power demand >=500 kW must report PUE, WUE, ERF, REF, cooling system details, and capacity to the EU database. First report due 15 September 2024; annual reports due 15 May thereafter. Portugal's national transposition of the full EED Recast (Directive 2023/1791) is pending as of April 2026.
regulations.relevance: Portuguese DCs >=500 kW must report PUE and cooling metrics to the EU database annually.

regulations.name: Decreto-Lei n.o 118/2013 — RECS (Regulamento de Desempenho Energetico dos Edificios de Comercio e Servicos)
regulations.summary: Part of the SCE framework, RECS sets minimum energy performance requirements for commercial and service buildings including mechanical ventilation, HVAC efficiency, lighting, and thermal envelope. New commercial buildings must meet nZEB standards. Technical HVAC systems must meet minimum seasonal efficiency requirements. Periodic inspections of HVAC systems >=12 kW cooling capacity are mandatory.
regulations.relevance: DC cooling systems >=12 kW are subject to periodic HVAC inspections. New DC buildings must meet nZEB standards under RECS.

regulations.name: Lei n.o 58/2005 — Lei da Agua (Water Law) and Decreto-Lei n.o 226-A/2007 (Water Use Regime)
regulations.summary: Portugal's Water Law transposes the EU Water Framework Directive. Decreto-Lei 226-A/2007 establishes the regime for utilization of water resources. Industrial water abstraction from public water domain (surface or groundwater) requires a utilization title (titulo de utilizacao) — either a licence or concession — from the Regional Hydrographic Authority (ARH, now integrated into APA - Agencia Portuguesa do Ambiente). Water use fees (Taxa de Recursos Hidricos, TRH) apply per m3.
regulations.relevance: DCs abstracting water for cooling from rivers, groundwater, or other public domain sources require a utilization title from APA. Closed-loop cooling on municipal supply requires only a supply contract.

regulations.name: Decreto-Lei n.o 75/2015 — Licenciamento Unico Ambiental (LUA)
regulations.summary: Establishes Portugal's Single Environmental Licensing regime (LUA), consolidating multiple environmental authorizations into a single process. Covers EIA (Avaliacao de Impacte Ambiental), integrated pollution prevention (PCIP/IPPC), waste management licensing, water discharge permits, and accident prevention. Applications are submitted through a single electronic portal. Large industrial installations falling under the IED (combustion >=50 MW thermal, etc.) require a Titulo Unico Ambiental (TUA).
regulations.relevance: The LUA streamlines environmental permitting into a single portal. Most standalone DCs fall below IED thresholds, but those with significant backup generation or industrial cooling discharge should verify. The single-portal approach reduces permitting complexity compared to other EU countries.

regulations.name: Codigo Fiscal do Investimento (Investment Tax Code) — RFAI (Regime Fiscal de Apoio ao Investimento) and SIFIDE (Sistema de Incentivos Fiscais em I&D)
regulations.summary: The Investment Tax Code (approved by Decreto-Lei 162/2014, consolidated) provides two key incentive regimes. RFAI offers a tax credit of 10-25% of eligible investment in tangible and intangible assets for productive investments in less-developed regions (aligned with EU regional aid map). SIFIDE provides a tax credit of 32.5% base rate + 50% incremental rate for R&D expenditure. Both regimes are available to DC operators making qualifying investments.
regulations.relevance: DC investments in Portugal may qualify for RFAI tax credits (10-25%) in eligible regions. R&D activities at DC sites (cooling innovation, AI research) may qualify for SIFIDE credits (32.5-50%).

regulations.name: EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364 — Direct Effect in Portugal (PUE Reporting)
regulations.summary: PUE reporting for Portuguese DCs >=500 kW is mandated directly by EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364. PUE must be calculated per EN 50600-4-2. No additional national PUE reporting obligation exists in Portugal beyond the EU requirement as of April 2026.
regulations.relevance: Annual PUE disclosure mandatory for DCs >=500 kW via EU database.

regulations.name: Energiegesetz (EnG) — Federal Energy Act (SR 730.0, 30 September 2016)
regulations.summary: Switzerland's Federal Energy Act sets the framework for energy policy including efficiency targets, renewable energy promotion, and large consumer obligations. Large energy consumers (Grossverbraucher) — enterprises consuming >500 MWh electricity/year — can enter into target agreements (Zielvereinbarungen) with the federal government or cantons to improve energy efficiency. Cantons must implement programmes for large consumers. The Act does not contain data-centre-specific provisions but data centres typically qualify as large consumers. Switzerland is NOT an EU member and is NOT subject to the EED or EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364.
regulations.relevance: DCs consuming >500 MWh/year (virtually all commercial DCs) are classified as Grossverbraucher and must participate in cantonal energy efficiency programmes or enter target agreements. No EU-mandated PUE reporting applies, but target agreements typically include energy intensity metrics.

regulations.name: Energieverordnung (EnV) — Federal Energy Ordinance (SR 730.01, as amended 2024)
regulations.summary: Implementing ordinance for the Energy Act. Specifies details of the large consumer programme, target agreement procedures, energy efficiency requirements for appliances and systems, and subsidy mechanisms. The 2024 amendment (AS 2024 702) updated efficiency standards and renewable energy provisions. Cantons retain authority to set additional requirements for large consumers.
regulations.relevance: Defines the operational framework for DC participation in large consumer efficiency programmes. Canton-level variation means DC operators must check local requirements.

regulations.name: Bundesgesetz uber die Reduktion der CO2-Emissionen (CO2 Act, revised 2024) and CO2-Verordnung (AS 2025 248)
regulations.summary: The revised CO2 Act (entered into force 1 January 2025) sets Switzerland's climate targets: net-zero by 2050, with interim targets of -50% by 2030 vs 1990. The CO2 Ordinance (AS 2025 248) implements the Act. Companies can be exempted from the CO2 levy if they commit to emission reduction targets. The Act covers building emissions, transport, and industry. DCs using fossil-fuel backup generators are subject to the CO2 levy on fuel purchases. Companies committing to reduction targets can recover the levy.
regulations.relevance: DCs operating diesel backup generators pay the CO2 levy (~120 CHF/tonne CO2). Target agreements can provide levy exemption. Heat recovery from DCs to district heating networks supports emission reduction commitments.

regulations.name: Raumplanungsgesetz (RPG) — Federal Spatial Planning Act (SR 700) and Cantonal Building Laws
regulations.summary: The Federal Spatial Planning Act sets the framework for land use planning. Building permits (Baubewilligungen) are governed by cantonal law — each of Switzerland's 26 cantons has its own building legislation. Federal law requires that construction conform to zoning plans and complies with environmental, fire safety, and structural requirements. DC construction in industrial or commercial zones typically requires a cantonal building permit, with processing times of 2-6 months depending on canton and project complexity.
regulations.relevance: DC construction requires cantonal building permits. Zoning restrictions vary significantly between cantons — proximity to residential areas, noise limits, and building height restrictions are cantonal matters.

regulations.name: Gewasserschutzgesetz (GSchG) — Federal Waters Protection Act (SR 814.20) and Gewasserschutzverordnung (GSchV, SR 814.201)
regulations.summary: The Federal Waters Protection Act and its implementing ordinance regulate water abstraction, discharge, and thermal pollution of watercourses. Industrial cooling water discharge to surface waters must not raise the receiving water temperature by more than 3C (or 1.5C in sensitive waters). Abstraction of groundwater or surface water for cooling requires a cantonal permit. The GSchV sets specific discharge quality standards. Cantons enforce the federal standards and may impose additional restrictions.
regulations.relevance: DCs using open-loop or evaporative cooling drawing from natural water sources need cantonal water permits. Thermal discharge limits (3C max temperature rise) are strictly enforced. Closed-loop liquid cooling on municipal water supply avoids most permitting requirements.

regulations.name: Umweltschutzgesetz (USG) — Federal Environmental Protection Act (SR 814.01) and UVPV (EIA Ordinance, SR 814.011)
regulations.summary: The Federal Environmental Protection Act establishes the framework for environmental impact assessment (UVP/EIA), air pollution control, noise protection, and waste management. The EIA Ordinance (UVPV) lists installations requiring an environmental impact assessment — large industrial plants, energy infrastructure, and certain commercial developments trigger EIA depending on size. DCs are not explicitly listed in the UVPV Annex, but very large facilities (>20 MW electrical) or those in sensitive locations may trigger cantonal EIA requirements. The Luftreinhalte-Verordnung (LRV, Air Pollution Control Ordinance) limits emissions from backup generators.
regulations.relevance: Most DCs do not trigger federal EIA, but cantonal thresholds vary. Backup generator emissions must comply with LRV limits. Noise limits from the USG apply to all DC sites and affect cooling equipment placement.

regulations.name: Cantonal Tax Incentives and Federal Gebauedeprogramm (Building Programme)
regulations.summary: Switzerland has no single federal DC tax incentive programme. However, several mechanisms apply: (1) The federal Gebaudeprogramm (Building Programme) subsidizes energy efficiency improvements in buildings, including commercial HVAC upgrades. (2) Cantons offer varying tax incentives — e.g., Canton of Zurich and Canton of Zug have favourable corporate tax rates (12-14% effective) attracting DC investment. (3) Companies participating in target agreements under the EnG can receive CO2 levy refunds. (4) ProKilowatt (competitive subsidy programme under EnG) funds energy efficiency projects including industrial cooling optimization.
regulations.relevance: DC operators can access ProKilowatt subsidies for cooling efficiency projects. Cantonal corporate tax rates (11-22% effective) are a significant factor in DC siting. CO2 levy refunds via target agreements reduce operating costs.

regulations.name: No Federal PUE Reporting Mandate — Voluntary via Target Agreements
regulations.summary: Switzerland does not impose a mandatory PUE reporting obligation. It is not subject to the EU EED or EU Delegated Regulation 2024/1364. However, DCs participating in target agreements (Zielvereinbarungen) under the Energy Act typically report energy intensity metrics including PUE-equivalent measures as part of their agreement. The Swiss Data Center Association (Swiss Datacenter Efficiency Association, SDEA) promotes voluntary PUE benchmarking among members.
regulations.relevance: No mandatory PUE reporting. Voluntary reporting through target agreements and industry association programmes. This represents lower regulatory burden compared to EU member states but less transparency for market intelligence.

regulations.name: Act on Rationalizing Energy Use and Shifting to Non-fossil Energy (Act No. 49, 1979; revised 2023)
regulations.summary: Japan's primary energy efficiency law, revised April 2023 to include non-fossil energy shifting obligations. Businesses consuming 1,500 kL or more crude-oil-equivalent energy per year are designated as 'Specified Business Operators' and must appoint an energy manager, submit annual energy consumption reports and medium-to-long-term energy rationalization plans to METI. Data centers fall under this regime as large energy consumers. The revised Act requires businesses to track and report non-fossil energy usage alongside rationalization efforts. Benchmarking system evaluates sector-specific energy efficiency with S/A/B/C ratings; businesses rated B or C face regulatory guidance.
regulations.relevance: Directly governs energy reporting obligations for data center operators in Japan. Cooling systems represent the largest non-IT energy load and are a primary target for rationalization plans. Operators must demonstrate year-over-year efficiency improvements including cooling plant optimization.

regulations.name: Act on Improvement of Energy Consumption Performance of Buildings (Building Energy Efficiency Act, Act No. 53, 2015; revised 2025)
regulations.summary: Originally enacted July 2015 with regulatory measures for large non-residential buildings. As of April 2025, compliance with energy efficiency standards is mandatory for all new buildings. Non-residential buildings above 2,000 m2 must meet energy consumption performance standards covering HVAC, lighting, hot water, and elevators. Incentive measures include labeling systems and expedited building permits for high-efficiency designs. Covers extensions, renovations, and improvements to cooling system equipment.
regulations.relevance: New data center construction must meet mandatory energy consumption performance standards. Cooling system design (chillers, air handling units, heat rejection equipment) is a major component of the building energy performance calculation. High-efficiency cooling designs may qualify for expedited permitting.

regulations.name: Environmental Impact Assessment Act (Act No. 81, 1997)
regulations.summary: Requires environmental impact assessment for large-scale development projects. Applicable to projects in categories defined by Cabinet Order including power plants, roads, railways, airports, and reclamation. While data centers are not explicitly listed as a designated project type, very large data center campus developments may trigger prefectural-level environmental assessment requirements depending on scale, location, and local ordinances.
regulations.relevance: Large data center campuses with on-site power generation or significant environmental footprint may require environmental impact assessment under prefectural ordinances. Cooling water discharge, noise from cooling towers, and thermal emissions are relevant assessment factors.

regulations.name: Water Pollution Control Law (Act No. 138, 1970)
regulations.summary: Regulates effluent from factories and business establishments discharged into public water bodies. National effluent standards apply to facilities discharging 50 m3 or more per day on average. Standards cover 24 substances for human health protection and 16 items for living environment protection. Prefectural governments may set stricter local standards. Cooling tower blowdown water from data centers must meet discharge standards for temperature, pH, and chemical treatment residuals.
regulations.relevance: Data centers using evaporative cooling towers must ensure blowdown water discharge meets national and prefectural effluent standards. Chemical water treatment (biocides, corrosion inhibitors) used in cooling water loops adds regulated substances to discharge. Closed-loop liquid cooling eliminates this compliance obligation.

regulations.name: GX Economy Transition Bonds and Strategic Tax Incentives (GX Promotion Act, Act No. 32, 2023)
regulations.summary: Under the GX Promotion Act, Japan will issue GX Economy Transition Bonds to provide approximately 20 trillion yen in advance investment support over 10 years for private sector decarbonization investments. Tax incentives include immediate depreciation for strategic investment areas and enhanced R&D tax credits for strategically important technologies. Data center energy efficiency investments and green cooling technology may qualify under the GX framework. Emissions trading system enters full operation FY2026; fossil fuel surcharge begins FY2028.
regulations.relevance: Data center operators investing in energy-efficient cooling technologies (liquid cooling, waste heat recovery, free cooling) may access GX tax incentives for decarbonization investments. Emissions trading from FY2026 adds cost to carbon-intensive cooling approaches using grid electricity.

regulations.name: Economic Security Promotion Act — Cloud/AI Infrastructure Subsidies
regulations.summary: Under Article 9(4) of the Economic Security Promotion Act, METI approved subsidies of up to 72.5 billion yen total to five projects for improving computational resources necessary for AI development. Targets domestic data center and cloud infrastructure investment to reduce reliance on foreign cloud providers (domestic share approximately 30% of basic cloud services market). Companies must establish infrastructure within Japan to ensure stable supply of designated critical goods.
